mission.title: Install and Configure Wasmedge Runtime on Kubernetes
mission.description: WasmEdge is a lightweight, high-performance WebAssembly runtime designed for cloud-native, edge, and decentralized applications. Installing WasmEdge allows you to run serverless apps, microservices, and smart contracts efficiently on various devices.

steps.title: Add the WasmEdge Helm repository
steps.description:
First, add the WasmEdge Helm repository to your Helm configuration:
```bash
helm repo add wasmedge https://wasmedge.org/charts
helm repo update
```

steps.title: Install WasmEdge using Helm
steps.description:
Now, install WasmEdge with the following command, creating a namespace if it doesn't exist:
```bash
helm install wasmedge wasmedge/wasmedge --namespace wasmedge --create-namespace --version 0.16.1
```

steps.title: Verify the installation
steps.description:
Check that the WasmEdge pods are running:
```bash
kubectl get pods --namespace wasmedge
```

steps.title: Configure resource limits
steps.description:
You may want to set resource limits for your WasmEdge deployment. Edit the deployment:
```bash
kubectl edit deployment wasmedge --namespace wasmedge
```
Add the following under `spec.containers.resources`:
```yaml
resources:
  limits:
    memory: "512Mi"
    cpu: "500m"
```

steps.title: Set up port forwarding for access
steps.description:
To access the WasmEdge service, set up port forwarding:
```bash
kubectl port-forward svc/wasmedge --namespace wasmedge 8080:80
```

uninstall.title: Remove the WasmEdge Helm release
uninstall.description:
Uninstall the WasmEdge release from your cluster:
```bash
helm uninstall wasmedge --namespace wasmedge
```

uninstall.title: Clean up CRDs and resources
uninstall.description:
Remove any custom resource definitions (CRDs) created by WasmEdge:
```bash
kubectl delete crd wasmedge.*
```

uninstall.title: Delete the WasmEdge namespace
uninstall.description:
Finally, delete the WasmEdge namespace to clean up:
```bash
kubectl delete namespace wasmedge
```

upgrade.title: Backup current state
upgrade.description:
Before upgrading, back up your current configuration and resources:
```bash
kubectl get all --namespace wasmedge -o yaml > wasmedge-backup.yaml
```

upgrade.title: Update the Helm repository
upgrade.description:
Ensure you have the latest charts from the WasmEdge repository:
```bash
helm repo update
```

upgrade.title: Upgrade WasmEdge
upgrade.description:
Perform the upgrade to the latest version:
```bash
helm upgrade wasmedge wasmedge/wasmedge --namespace wasmedge --version 0.16.1
```

upgrade.title: Verify the upgrade
upgrade.description:
Check that the WasmEdge pods are running after the upgrade:
```bash
kubectl get pods --namespace wasmedge
```

troubleshooting.title: Pods stuck in Pending
troubleshooting.description:
If your WasmEdge pods are stuck in Pending, check for resource availability:
```bash
kubectl describe pod <pod-name> --namespace wasmedge
```
Ensure your cluster has enough resources (CPU/memory) available.

troubleshooting.title: Service not reachable
troubleshooting.description:
If you cannot access the WasmEdge service, verify the service status:
```bash
kubectl get svc --namespace wasmedge
```
Ensure the service is correctly configured and check the port forwarding command.

troubleshooting.title: CrashLoopBackOff errors
troubleshooting.description:
If the WasmEdge pods are in a CrashLoopBackOff state, check the logs:
```bash
kubectl logs <pod-name> --namespace wasmedge
```
Look for any error messages that indicate what might be wrong with the application.

resolution.summary: A successful installation of WasmEdge will have all pods running in the 'wasmedge' namespace, and you will be able to access the service via the specified port. Resource limits should be configured as needed.
codeSnippets:
helm repo add wasmedge https://wasmedge.org/charts
helm repo update

prerequisites.description: Ensure you have Kubernetes cluster access and Helm installed.
